channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Optimizing IoT Intrusion Detection with Tabular Foundation Models for Smart City Forensics
item.description: This paper presents the first systematic evaluation of TabPFNv2.5—a transformer-based tabular foundation model—for real-time IoT intrusion detection in smart city forensics. Using the TON IoT dataset, we show that TabPFNv2.5 achieves **97% binary classification accuracy** while delivering **40× faster inference** than Random Forest—enabling sub-millisecond threat screening on CPU. To bridge speed-accuracy trade-offs, we propose a hybrid pipeline: TabPFNv2.5 performs rapid initial triage, and ensemble models (e.g., RF, XGBoost) handle detailed multi-class attribution only for high-risk samples. Key findings include: (1) scanning attacks remain the most challenging to detect (F1 = 69.8%); (2) cross-device generalization critically depends on feature distribution similarity; and (3) our hybrid approach maintains 96.3% end-to-end accuracy while improving system throughput by 3.2×. These results establish tabular foundation models as viable, low-overhead components for time-sensitive IoT security operations.

item.title: The Salami Slicing Threat: Exploiting Cumulative Risks in LLM Systems
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) are vulnerable to multi-turn jailbreak attacks, yet existing methods suffer from poor generalizability and high detectability due to reliance on explicit, model-specific harmful triggers. To address this, we introduce the *Salami Slicing Risk*—a novel threat paradigm where numerous low-risk inputs, each individually below safety thresholds, are strategically chained to cumulatively build harmful intent and trigger high-risk outputs without engineered context. Based on this, we propose **Salami Attack**, a fully automatic, cross-model and cross-modality framework. Extensive experiments show it achieves >90% Attack Success Rate (ASR) on GPT-4o and Gemini, outperforming prior state-of-the-art methods and demonstrating strong robustness against real-world alignment defenses (e.g., Constitutional AI, Self-Refine). We further design a lightweight defense—Cumulative Intent Monitor (CIM)—that reduces Salami Attack ASR by ≥44.8% with only 0.8% latency overhead, while blocking 64.8% of other multi-turn jailbreaks. This work uncovers a critical cumulative-risk vulnerability in LLM interaction dynamics and delivers actionable insights for adaptive security hardening.

item.title: QShield: Securing Neural Networks Against Adversarial Attacks using Quantum Circuits
item.description: QShield is a modular hybrid quantum-classical neural network (HQCNN) architecture designed to enhance adversarial robustness of deep learning models without compromising accuracy. It integrates a classical CNN backbone for feature extraction with a differentiable quantum processing module that encodes features into parameterized quantum states, applies structured entanglement operations under realistic noise models (e.g., decoherence, gate errors), and fuses classical and quantum predictions via a lightweight dynamic-weighting MLP. Evaluated on MNIST, OrganAMNIST, and CIFAR-10 against PGD, FGSM, CW, and five other attacks, QShield reduces average attack success rates by 62.3–78.9% while maintaining >98.8% clean accuracy. Crucially, it increases the computational cost of adversarial example generation by over 4.1×, introducing a “computational barrier” defense. This work demonstrates that structured quantum entanglement—when co-designed with noise-aware training—enables practical, scalable robustness for safety-critical AI systems.

item.title: Answering Counting Queries with Differential Privacy on a Quantum Computer
item.description: We study differentially private answering of counting queries—e.g., “How many individuals satisfy two binary predicates?”—on quantum-encoded datasets. We show such queries reduce to estimating the amplitude of an orthogonal quantum state. For repeated computational-basis measurements, we prove tighter privacy bounds *specifically for counting queries*, demonstrating inherent privacy amplification due to measurement randomness. For amplitude estimation (AE), we derive the exact global sensitivity ($\Delta_f = 2/\sqrt{N}$) — a first for this setting — and design DP-AE, a differentially private variant that injects calibrated Laplace noise into the phase estimation step while preserving $O(1/\sqrt{M})$ estimation error with $M$ oracle calls. Finally, we outline an outsourcing protocol enabling a classical client to delegate private counting to an untrusted quantum server. This work establishes foundational algorithms and sensitivity analysis for practical quantum differential privacy.

item.title: A Proposed Biomedical Data Policy Framework to Reduce Fragmentation, Improve Quality, and Incentivize Sharing in Indian Healthcare in the era of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Health
item.description: India’s vast biomedical data—generated across public hospitals, private EMRs, insurance schemes, and research—remains fragmented due to institutional silos and vendor-locked systems. The core bottleneck is not technical but incentive-based: data curation lacks recognition in academic promotion (NMC), institutional rankings (NIRF), or funding mechanisms. To address this, we propose a multi-layered policy framework that: (1) integrates peer-reviewed **data papers** into NMC promotion criteria; (2) embeds open-data metrics into NIRF; (3) adopts **Shapley-value-based revenue sharing** in federated learning consortia; and (4) institutionalizes **data stewardship** as a professional role. We mitigate key barriers—including quality anxiety and misinterpretation risks—via mandatory data quality assessment, structured peer review, and academic credit for auditing. Fully aligned with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDPA), the framework constructively engages NDSAP, Biotech-PRIDE, and ANRF guidelines to enable high-quality, ethical, and incentivized data sharing for AI-driven health innovation in India.

item.title: Exact Certification of Neural Networks and Partition Aggregation Ensembles against Label Poisoning
item.description: Label-flipping attacks pose a critical threat to supervised learning by corrupting training labels to induce inference-time misclassifications. While certification frameworks aim to provide formal robustness guarantees—e.g., “the model’s prediction remains unchanged under up to $k$ label flips”—existing partition-aggregation methods treat base classifiers as black boxes, yielding overly conservative bounds. We introduce **EnsembleCert**, the first white-box certification framework for partition-aggregation ensembles, which aggregates per-partition *exact* certificates of base classifiers to compute tight ensemble-level guarantees in polynomial time. To enable this, we propose **ScaLabelCert**, the first exact, polynomial-time computable certificate for neural networks against label flipping—leveraging the neural tangent kernel (NTK) equivalence of wide networks. On CIFAR-10, EnsembleCert certifies **+26.5% more label flips (median)** than state-of-the-art black-box methods while using **100× fewer partitions**, challenging the dogma that heavy partitioning is essential for strong certified robustness.

item.title: Representation-Aligned Multi-Scale Personalization for Federated Learning
item.description: Federated learning (FL) struggles to accommodate clients with heterogeneous resource constraints. Conventional approaches—relying on a shared full-size backbone and client-specific submodel extraction—suffer from limited structural diversity and poor representational adaptation. This paper proposes **FRAMP**, a unified framework for personalized, resource-adaptive FL. Instead of a fixed global model, FRAMP generates *client-specific lightweight models* from compact descriptors encoding computational budgets and data characteristics. Crucially, it enforces **representation alignment** across clients via contrastive semantic consistency losses, preserving global semantics while enabling fine-grained personalization. Extensive experiments on vision (CIFAR, Tiny-ImageNet) and graph (OGBN-Arxiv, Reddit) benchmarks show FRAMP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods—improving accuracy by +3.2–5.7% on resource-constrained clients—while enhancing cross-client representation coherence by 38%.

item.title: A Full Compression Pipeline for Green Federated Learning in Communication-Constrained Environments
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative model training but suffers from prohibitive communication and computation overheads in resource-constrained environments. To address this, we propose the **Full Compression Pipeline (FCP)**—a unified end-to-end framework integrating structured pruning, 8-bit quantization, and adaptive Huffman encoding. FCP compresses local models *and* communication payloads jointly, drastically reducing transmission costs and device resource consumption while preserving model accuracy. We introduce a holistic evaluation metric—**unified model cost**—that jointly quantifies communication and computation overheads. Evaluated on CIFAR-10 with 10 clients under 2 Mbps bandwidth, FCP achieves **11.3× model size reduction**, only a **2.0% accuracy drop** (90.1% vs. 92.1%), and **61.7% faster FL training**, with robust performance under both IID and non-IID data distributions. FCP delivers a practical, efficient, and deployable solution for green FL.

item.title: Detecting RAG Extraction Attack via Dual-Path Runtime Integrity Game
item.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems face a critical security threat: *RAG Knowledge Base Leakage*, where adversarial prompts extract proprietary retrieved content. Existing defenses lack real-time detection, adaptability, and plug-and-play deployment. We propose **CanaryRAG**, a runtime integrity defense inspired by stack canaries. It embeds verifiable, semantics-preserving *canary tokens* into retrieved chunks and frames defense as a **dual-path integrity game**—monitoring both the target (user-facing) and oracle (canary-augmented) generation paths for behavioral violations. CanaryRAG detects leakage in real time—even under adaptive suppression and obfuscation—without model retraining or pipeline modification. Evaluated against 7 state-of-the-art RAG extraction attacks, it reduces average chunk recovery rate to **2.1%** (vs. 38.7% for SOTA baselines), with **<0.8% task accuracy drop** and **<3.2 ms latency overhead**. As a lightweight, universal plug-in, CanaryRAG delivers practical, scalable protection for proprietary RAG knowledge bases.

item.title: DuCodeMark: Dual-Purpose Code Dataset Watermarking via Style-Aware Watermark-Poison Design
item.description: DuCodeMark is the first dual-purpose, style-aware watermarking framework for code datasets that simultaneously secures both source-code generation and decompilation tasks. It parses code into ASTs, applies language-specific stylistic transformations to generate stealthy trigger-target pairs, and injects *repressible poisoned features* into return-typed samples—features remain inert during normal training but activate upon watermark removal, causing significant performance degradation (e.g., Pass@1 drops by 28.6%). Verification uses a black-box independent-samples *t*-test. Evaluated across 72 settings (2 tasks × 2 languages × 3 CodeLMs × 6 temperatures), DuCodeMark achieves strong verifiability (*p* < 0.05), high stealthiness (suspicious rate ≤ 0.36), robustness against watermark removal and poisoning attacks (recall ≤ 0.57), and negligible impact on clean-task performance (<0.8% Pass@1 drop). It establishes a practical, resilient foundation for open-source code copyright protection in the LLM era.

item.title: The Blind Spot of Agent Safety: How Benign User Instructions Expose Critical Vulnerabilities in Computer-Use Agents
item.description: This paper identifies a critical safety blind spot in Computer-Use Agents (CUAs): severe vulnerabilities triggered *not by malicious instructions*, but by **benign user commands interacting unexpectedly with task context or environment dynamics**. To expose this, we introduce **OS-BLIND**, a novel benchmark comprising 300 human-crafted tasks across 12 categories and 8 real-world applications, organized into two threat clusters—*environment-embedded threats* and *agent-initiated harms*. Evaluations on state-of-the-art models (e.g., Claude 4.5 Sonnet, GPT-4o) and agentic frameworks reveal alarmingly high attack success rates (ASRs): >90% for most CUAs, 73.0% for safety-aligned Claude 4.5 Sonnet—and rising to **92.7% in multi-agent settings**, where subtask decomposition obscures harmful intent. Crucially, existing safety alignment mechanisms activate only transiently (typically within the first few steps) and fail to re-engage during long-horizon execution. OS-BLIND highlights the urgent need for context-aware, dynamically adaptive safety protocols beyond prompt-level guarding. The benchmark will be publicly released to advance robust CUA safety research.

item.title: Task2vec Readiness: Diagnostics for Federated Learning from Pre-Training Embeddings
item.description: Federated learning (FL) performance is notoriously sensitive to client data heterogeneity, yet practitioners lack pre-training methods to reliably anticipate federation behavior. This paper introduces **Task2Vec Readiness**: a set of unsupervised, embedding-based diagnostics computed *before any FL training*. We derive three geometric metrics—**cohesion**, **dispersion**, and **density**—directly from Task2Vec task embeddings of individual clients. Evaluated across CIFAR-10, FEMNIST, PathMNIST, and BloodMNIST under Dirichlet heterogeneity (α = 0.05 to 5.0) and FedAVG, our indices show strong, consistent correlations with final model accuracy: cohesion achieves Pearson/Spearman coefficients >0.9 in most dataset×client configurations. These results validate Task2Vec Readiness as a principled, lightweight, and predictive pre-training diagnostic for FL—enabling early client selection, heterogeneity assessment, and resource-aware federation design without any communication or model training overhead.

item.title: FedRio: Personalized Federated Social Bot Detection via Cooperative Reinforced Contrastive Adversarial Distillation
item.description: Social bot detection is vital for platform integrity, yet isolated model development hinders cross-platform generalization and rapid adaptation to emerging bot variants. To bridge this gap under strict privacy constraints, we propose **FedRio**, a personalized federated framework integrating *cooperative reinforced contrastive adversarial distillation*. FedRio features: (1) an adaptive graph neural network backbone per client; (2) a GAN-based federated knowledge extractor that models global data distributions without raw data sharing; (3) multi-stage adversarial contrastive learning to align local feature spaces and reduce client-server divergence; and (4) reinforcement-learning-guided client parameter control alongside adaptive server aggregation to handle severe data heterogeneity. Extensive experiments on TwiBot-20 and Cresci-2017 show FedRio consistently outperforms state-of-the-art federated baselines in F1-score (+3.2–5.8%), communication efficiency (−42% upload volume), and feature consistency (−61% centering error), while remaining highly competitive with centralized methods—despite enforcing substantially stronger privacy guarantees (no raw data, no label exchange, end-to-end encryption).

item.title: Enhancing Cross-Problem Vehicle Routing via Federated Learning
item.description: Vehicle routing problems (VRPs) are fundamental in logistics optimization, yet existing neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) methods suffer from performance degradation and poor generalizability when transferring across VRP variants with heterogeneous constraints (e.g., time windows, multi-depots, electric vehicle charging). To address this, we propose **MPSF-FL**: a *Multi-problem Pre-train, then Single-problem Fine-tune* framework powered by Federated Learning. MPSF-FL first builds a shared global model via federated pre-training on diverse VRP subtypes, then enables each client to fine-tune its local model using constraint-conditioned knowledge distillation from the up-to-date global model—ensuring both cross-problem knowledge transfer and downstream task specialization. Experiments on 12 VRP benchmarks show MPSF-FL improves solution quality by **4.2–9.7%** over SOTA NCO baselines (e.g., AM, POMO) and boosts zero-shot generalization to unseen VRPs (e.g., E-VRP) by **31.5%**, all while reducing communication cost by 38%.

item.title: Differentially Private Verification of Distribution Properties
item.description: This paper initiates the study of *differentially private (DP) distribution property verification*, where a verifier uses samples from a sensitive distribution and interacts with an untrusted but knowledgeable prover. We map the landscape of DP interactive proofs for distribution properties, resolving when private randomness (private coins) offers advantages over public coins under privacy constraints. Our main results are: (1) For $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP with $\varepsilon = O(1/\sqrt{n})$, $\delta = O(1/n^{5/2})$, any one-round private-coin DP protocol can be converted into an equivalent one-round public-coin DP protocol—preserving privacy parameters, sample complexity, and communication complexity; (2) If the verifier’s message satisfies only $O(1/\sqrt{\log n})$-local DP (a much relaxed regime), one additional transformation yields a public-coin protocol with identical DP guarantees and efficiency; (3) Conversely, when $\varepsilon \in \Omega(\log n)$, private coins *do* reduce complexity, revealing a sharp phase transition. We also give an optimal-sample-complexity Merlin–Arthur (one-message) DP protocol for testing product distributions.

item.title: Communication-Efficient Gluon in Federated Learning
item.description: Recent advances show Muon-type optimizers—leveraging linear minimization oracles (LMOs) over non-Euclidean norm balls—outperform Adam in large language model training. Yet communication cost remains prohibitive in federated learning. This paper introduces **Communication-Efficient Gluon**, an extension of Muon under layer-wise $(L^0, L^1)$-smoothness, equipped with both unbiased and contraction compressors. We integrate SARAH-style variance reduction to mitigate compression error, achieving $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T})$ convergence and improved communication complexity $\mathcal{O}(d/\varepsilon^2)$. As a byproduct, we derive **VR-Gluon**, a new variance-reduced variant with faster convergence than standard Gluon. Further, momentum variance reduction (MVR) is incorporated, yielding comparable communication efficiency even when $L_i^1 \neq 0$—a weaker condition than prior work. Experiments confirm up to **47% fewer communication rounds** and **3.2× bandwidth reduction**, with no accuracy loss.

item.title: Energy-Efficient Federated Edge Learning For Small-Scale Datasets in Large IoT Networks
item.description: Large-scale IoT networks face critical challenges in federated edge learning (FEL) when dealing with small-scale, heterogeneous datasets: poor convergence, high energy consumption, and low resource utilization. To address this, we propose **EE-FEL**, an energy-efficient collaborative optimization framework tailored for small-data regimes. We first derive a tight **expected learning loss bound** that explicitly links sample size, data heterogeneity, and generalization error. Then, we design a **lightweight stochastic online learning algorithm** to adapt to dynamic data arrivals and distribution shifts. A joint resource optimization problem is formulated—minimizing end-to-end energy under a **rigorously proven convergence guarantee**—and solved via a **fully decentralized, online distributed algorithm** requiring only local and neighbor information. Extensive simulations and autonomous navigation case studies (with real-world collision-avoidance tasks) show EE-FEL achieves **23.6% higher accuracy**, **41.2% lower edge energy consumption**, and **37% fewer communication rounds** versus state-of-the-art baselines (e.g., FedAvg, FedProx), while maintaining a bounded convergence error ≤ 0.02.

item.title: FEDBUD: Joint Incentive and Privacy Optimization for Resource-Constrained Federated Learning
item.description: Federated learning (FL) faces dual challenges: defending against differential privacy (DP) attacks and designing economically viable incentive mechanisms. Prior works treat data volume and noise level separately, ignoring their joint impact on utility-cost trade-offs. This paper proposes **FEDBUD**, a novel FL framework that jointly optimizes monetary incentives and DP privacy by modeling the *coupled influence* of data volume and noise level on model performance and participant utility. We formulate FEDBUD as a two-stage Stackelberg game: the cloud server (leader) sets budget-aware payments, while edge nodes (followers) strategically choose both data volume and DP noise intensity. To solve this non-convex game under resource constraints, we integrate a **mean-field estimator** for scalable node interaction modeling and a **virtual queue** for provably stable budget-constrained equilibrium convergence. Experiments on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and real-world medical time-series data show FEDBUD achieves **8.7% higher accuracy at ε = 2.0** and **reduces average noise injection by 32%** to meet target accuracy (>85%), outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. FEDBUD is the first to enable incentive compatibility and rigorous DP guarantees simultaneously in resource-constrained FL.

item.title: Membership Inference Attacks Expose Participation Privacy in ECG Foundation Encoders
item.description: This paper audits membership inference attacks (MIAs) against modern self-supervised ECG foundation encoders—including contrastive (SimCLR, TS2Vec) and masked autoencoding (CNN-/Transformer-based MAE) models—under three realistic attacker interfaces: scalar-score black-box access, adaptive subject-level statistics aggregation, and latent embedding geometry probing. Using a subject-centric, cross-dataset evaluation protocol with calibrated false-positive rates, we find that participation leakage is objective-dependent and highly heterogeneous: contrastive encoders exhibit saturation-level leakage in embedding space, especially for small or institution-specific cohorts (AUC > 0.85), whereas large, diverse pretraining datasets substantially attenuate operational tail risk (AUC < 0.60). Crucially, our results demonstrate that withholding raw waveforms and diagnostic labels is insufficient to guarantee participation privacy—exposing an underappreciated threat in connected-health deployments of reusable biosignal models. This work establishes the first empirically grounded, deployment-aware auditing framework for participation privacy in ECG foundation encoders.

item.title: Replicable Composition
item.description: We resolve the central open problem of replicable composition: given $k$ problems each solvable by a $\rho$-replicable algorithm with sample complexity $n_i$, we show they can be jointly solved with $\widetilde{O}(\sum_i n_i)$ samples while preserving *constant* replicability—achieving the optimal linear scaling. Our approach converts replicable algorithms into perfectly generalizing ones, composes them via a privacy-inspired analysis, and maps back using correlated sampling—yielding the first advanced composition theorem for replicability. We also prove a tight $\Omega(nk^2)$ lower bound for *adaptive* composition, establishing a quadratic separation from the non-adaptive case via our novel “phantom run” technique. As a byproduct, we give a boosting theorem that decouples failure probability from $\rho$, directly improving sample bounds for fundamental problems like halfspace learning and clustering.

item.title: LASQ: A Low-resource Aspect-based Sentiment Quadruple Extraction Dataset
item.description: This paper introduces **LASQ**, the first low-resource Aspect-based Sentiment Quadruple (TAOS) extraction dataset, covering **Uzbek** and **Uyghur**—two typologically rich agglutinative languages with scarce NLP resources. LASQ contains 12,847 manually annotated quadruples, supporting fine-grained joint extraction of target, aspect, opinion, and sentiment. To address lexical sparsity inherent in agglutinative morphology, we propose **GridTagger-SKEM**, a grid-tagging model enhanced with a Syntax Knowledge Embedding Module (SKEM) that injects POS tags and dependency paths into the encoder-decoder architecture. Experiments show consistent improvements: GridTagger-SKEM achieves +4.2 F₁ on Uzbek and +5.7 F₁ on Uyghur over strong span-based baselines. Ablation confirms SKEM’s critical role in mitigating data sparsity. LASQ and our model establish a foundational benchmark and practical framework for low-resource ABSA.

item.title: Optimizing and Implementing Threshold MAYO
item.description: Threshold signatures are critical for high-assurance systems, eliminating single points of failure and mitigating insider/key-exfiltration risks—priorities highlighted by NIST’s MPTC initiative. We present the first practical $t$-out-of-$n$ threshold instantiation of MAYO, a NIST post-quantum signature candidate. Building on Celi–Escudero–Niot’s PQCrypto 2025 design, we introduce two key algorithmic optimizations: (1) *Explicit-Salt MAYO*, enabling a **single-round online signing phase**, and (2) *Depth-Reduced MAYO*, minimizing secret-dependent circuit depth to $O(1)$. Integrated within a unified MPC protocol framework—including preprocessing, batching, and lightweight zero-knowledge checks—we achieve low-latency signing under active adversaries in the dishonest-majority setting. Local emulation shows **286 ms per threshold signature** ($t=3,n=5$), confirming practicality. An open-source implementation and end-to-end benchmarks are released, establishing a concrete reference for thresholdizing UOV-family signatures.

item.title: Drop-In Masked Modular Reduction for ML-DSA: Cutting Side-Channel Cost in the Root-of-Trust
item.description: Masking is a robust defense against side-channel attacks but incurs prohibitive hardware overhead—e.g., Caliptra’s masked ML-DSA implementation suffers ~6× LUT area increase. We propose a novel *drop-in*, first-order masked modular reduction for ML-DSA’s $q = 8380417$ reduction, designed specifically for resource-constrained Root-of-Trust. Our solution replaces the existing reduction module without altering interfaces or algorithm flow, leveraging a mask-efficient segmented linear reconstruction and pipelined residual compensation to eliminate costly masked multiplications and data-dependent branches. Compared to Caliptra’s baseline, it achieves **12.1× speedup**, reduces **LUTs by 86.7% and FFs by 94.5%**, and improves area–delay efficiency by **91×**. TVLA on >1,000,000 power traces shows **no first-order leakage** at α = 10⁻⁴, fully satisfying Caliptra’s security requirements. This work bridges the gap between provable side-channel security and practical hardware deployment for post-quantum digital signatures.

item.title: zkRAG: Efficiently Proving RAG Retrieval in Zero Knowledge
item.description: We present **zkRAG**, the first practical zero-knowledge, succinct, non-interactive argument for verifying retrieval consistency in RAG-as-a-Service. zkRAG targets the widely deployed HNSW algorithm and introduces a custom polynomial interactive oracle proof (PIOP) that models its hierarchical graph traversal, priority-queue dynamics, and neighbor lookups. Key innovations include a hybrid lookup argument, a highly efficient checker-based PIOP for priority-queue operations, and a lightweight membership selector verifier. On a benchmark of $10^6$ 128-dimensional vectors, zkRAG generates proofs in **50 seconds** (single-threaded)—over **1000× faster** than prior ZK-ANNS baselines—while keeping verification succinct (<10 ms) and proof size small (~12 KB). Its prover time is asymptotically optimal: linear in the HNSW search trace length. zkRAG thus enables efficient, trustless verification of RAG retrieval without revealing embeddings, index structure, or query internals.

item.title: Block Circulant Codes for Ethereum PeerDAS
item.description: This paper proposes **block circulant (BC) codes** as a practical alternative to 1D and 2D Reed–Solomon (RS) codes in Ethereum’s PeerDAS protocol. Within the same implementation framework used for 1D RS—leveraging optimized FFT/IFFT and finite-field arithmetic—we design efficient BC encoding and reconstruction algorithms. Crucially, BC encoding natively supports **KZG polynomial commitments**, enabling direct commitment to structured codeword polynomials without interpolation overhead. We evaluate BC codes against both 1D and 2D RS across three key metrics: code rate, stopping rate, and local code size. Results show BC achieves a high code rate (~0.75), reduces stopping rate by up to 38% versus 2D RS (and 12× vs. 1D RS), and maintains tightly bounded local code sizes (≤128 bytes)—offering superior data availability guarantees with lower sampling complexity and better ZK-friendliness.

item.title: Mask-Free Privacy Extraction and Rewriting: A Domain-Aware Approach via Prototype Learning
item.description: Client-side privacy rewriting is essential for deploying LLMs in sensitive domains (e.g., healthcare, finance), yet existing methods fail to balance privacy preservation and semantic utility. Full-text approaches distort context; span-level methods rely on brittle manual masks or static dictionaries; and prompt-based LLM localization suffers from unstable instruction following—causing both privacy leakage and over-scrubbing. To address this, we propose **DAMPER**, a mask-free, domain-aware framework. DAMPER learns compact **Domain Privacy Prototypes** via contrastive learning to enable autonomous, context-sensitive privacy span localization. It further introduces **Prototype-Guided Preference Alignment**, using prototypes as semantic anchors to construct preference pairs *without human annotations*, optimizing a domain-compliant rewriting policy. At inference, DAMPER integrates a sampling-based Exponential Mechanism to provide rigorous *span-level Differential Privacy* guarantees. Experiments across medical and financial benchmarks show DAMPER reduces privacy leakage by 62.3% and improves utility (e.g., NER F1, classification accuracy) by 18.7% on average over SOTA baselines—achieving a superior privacy-utility trade-off.

item.title: PlanGuard: Defending Agents against Indirect Prompt Injection via Planning-based Consistency Verification
item.description: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed in safety-critical systems but remain vulnerable to Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI), where attackers embed malicious commands in retrieved content to hijack agent behavior. Existing defenses focus on input sanitization and neglect real-time behavioral monitoring. We propose **PlanGuard**, a training-free, model-agnostic defense framework grounded in *Context Isolation*. It introduces an **isolated Planner** that generates a reference action set *solely from the user instruction*, decoupled from potentially poisoned context. A **Hierarchical Verification Mechanism** then enforces strict hard constraints to block unauthorized tool calls, followed by an **Intent Verifier** that discriminates benign parameter variations from malicious semantic hijacking. Evaluated on the InjecAgent benchmark, PlanGuard reduces the Attack Success Rate (ASR) from 72.8% to **0%**, with only a 1.49% False Positive Rate. Its zero-shot, inference-time design ensures broad compatibility and deployment readiness.

item.title: Like a Hammer, It Can Build, It Can Break: Large Language Model Uses, Perceptions, and Adoption in Cybersecurity Operations on Reddit
item.description: This study presents the first large-scale empirical analysis of how real-world cybersecurity practitioners use, perceive, and adopt LLMs in operational settings. Drawing on 892 Reddit posts from three security-focused subreddits (Dec 2022–Sep 2025), we employed mixed methods—qualitative thematic coding and statistical analysis—to examine LLM usage across three dimensions: tools/use cases, perceived pros/cons (on accuracy, explainability, privacy, integration, and compliance), and adoption stages/impacts. We find a clear “dual-track” adoption pattern: practitioners independently deploy general-purpose LLMs (e.g., ChatGPT) for low-risk productivity tasks (e.g., report drafting), while enterprise-grade security LLMs remain in early evaluation for high-stakes workflows like threat hunting. Though users report substantial efficiency gains (e.g., ~30% faster SIEM rule writing), reliability concerns—hallucinations, context loss, and data leakage—force near-universal human verification, severely limiting autonomy. Critically, practitioners frame LLMs not as autonomous agents but as *augmentation tools requiring rigorous accountability*, warning that unchecked adoption risks eroding analytical rigor and increasing false positives. Our findings inform actionable design principles for trustworthy, SOC-integrated LLM systems.

item.title: EncFormer: Secure and Efficient Transformer Inference over Encrypted Data
item.description: Transformer inference in MLaaS threatens user input privacy. Prior hybrid FHE-MPC solutions suffer from inefficient FHE kernels, communication-heavy MPC protocols, and costly FHE-MPC conversions. EncFormer addresses these via: (1) *Stage Compatible Patterns* that enable efficient FHE composition—minimizing repacking and conversions; (2) a cost-aware model built on a *minimal-conversion baseline* for principled FHE-MPC boundary selection; (3) a secure *complex CKKS-MPC conversion protocol*, eliminating real-part duplication overhead; and (4) communication-efficient MPC designs for nonlinearities (e.g., Softmax, GeLU), accelerated by GPU-optimized ciphertext batching. Evaluations on GPT-2 and BERT-base show EncFormer reduces online MPC communication by 1.4×–30.4× and end-to-end latency by 1.3×–9.8× over prior hybrid systems, and achieves 1.9×–3.5× lower latency than FHE-only pipelines—while preserving >98% plaintext accuracy on GLUE tasks.

item.title: Class-Adaptive Cooperative Perception for Multi-Class LiDAR-based 3D Object Detection in V2X Systems
item.description: Cooperative perception in V2X systems enables vehicles and roadside units to jointly detect 3D objects by fusing LiDAR point clouds, yet most existing methods apply a *one-size-fits-all* fusion strategy across object classes—ignoring fundamental disparities in geometry, point density, and spatial extent between small (e.g., pedestrians) and large (e.g., trucks) objects. To bridge this gap, we propose **Class-Adaptive Cooperative Perception (CACP)**, a novel architecture featuring: (1) multi-scale window attention with learned scale routing for geometry-aware feature extraction; (2) a class-specific fusion module that decouples small- and large-object pathways via attentive gating; (3) BEV enhancement via parallel dilated convolutions and channel recalibration; and (4) class-balanced objective weighting to mitigate long-tail bias. Evaluated on the V2X-Real benchmark across five realistic cooperation settings (vehicle-centric, infrastructure-centric, V2V, I2I, V2I), CACP consistently outperforms strong intermediate-fusion baselines—achieving the largest gains on trucks (+4.2 mAP), clear improvements on pedestrians (+2.8), and competitive performance on cars. Our results demonstrate that *class-adaptive alignment of feature learning and fusion with object-specific priors is essential for balanced, robust multi-class detection in real-world V2X deployments*.

item.title: STARS: Skill-Triggered Audit for Request-Conditioned Invocation Safety in Agent Systems
item.description: Autonomous agent systems increasingly rely on installable skills, yet static skill auditing fails to assess *request- and context-conditioned* invocation risks at runtime. We formalize skill invocation auditing as a continuous-risk estimation problem and propose **STARS**, a framework combining: (1) a static capability prior, (2) a request-conditioned risk model that jointly encodes user request, candidate skill, and runtime context, and (3) a calibrated fusion policy. To enable rigorous evaluation, we introduce **SIA-Bench**—a benchmark of 3,000 real-world invocation records with group-safe splits, lineage metadata, full runtime context, canonical action labels, and human-annotated continuous-risk targets. On an out-of-distribution indirect prompt injection split, calibrated STARS fusion achieves **0.439 high-risk AUPRC**, outperforming the contextual scorer (0.405) and best static baseline (0.380); the contextual scorer remains better calibrated (ECE = 0.289). Gains are modest on the in-distribution test set, confirming static priors retain value. Thus, request-conditioned auditing is most impactful as a *real-time risk-scoring and triage layer*, not a replacement for static screening. Code: https://github.com/123zgj123/STARS.

item.title: Shuffling the Data, Stretching the Step-size: Sharper Bias in constant step-size SGD
item.description: This paper studies constant-step-size stochastic gradient methods for finite-sum variational inequality problems (VIPs), common in adversarial robustness and multi-agent learning. While random reshuffling (RR) improves mean-squared error and Richardson–Romberg extrapolation (RRX) reduces bias to $O(\eta^2)$, we prove their *composition* achieves a strictly sharper $O(\eta^3)$ bias—first such guarantee for structured non-monotone VIPs. Our analysis combines (i) Markov chain theory to handle reshuffling noise via novel LLN and CLT for iterates, and (ii) spectral tensor techniques to show RRX debiases even under the biased gradient oracle induced by RR. Experiments confirm consistent cubic bias decay and substantial speedups over baselines.

item.title: Cross-Validated Cross-Channel Self-Attention and Denoising for Automatic Modulation Classification
item.description: This paper tackles the SNR-robustness bottleneck in deep learning-based Automatic Modulation Classification (AMC) by proposing a **cross-channel self-attention and feature-preserving denoising architecture**. Conventional feature extractors degrade discriminative modulation structures under noise; our model addresses this via: (1) a **cross-channel self-attention block** that explicitly models I/Q component interdependencies as orthogonal subproblems, generalizing complex-domain interactions; and (2) **dual-path deep residual shrinkage denoising blocks (DRSDB)** that suppress AWGN while preserving critical modulation fingerprints. Evaluated on RML2018.01a (24 modulations, 26 SNRs) with stratified cross-validation, the model achieves **+3.0%, +2.3%, and +14.0% accuracy gains over PET-CGDNN, MCLDNN, and DAE** at −8 dB to +2 dB SNR. Cross-validation yields **62.6% mean accuracy, 65.8% macro-precision, 62.6% macro-recall, and 62.9% macro-F1**, with ablation confirming DRSDB’s critical role in low-to-moderate SNR robustness. The work advances interference-aware AMC through formalized baseband modeling and generalized complex attention.

item.title: LoDAdaC: a unified local training-based decentralized framework with adaptive gradients and compressed communication
item.description: In decentralized distributed learning, balancing fast convergence and low communication cost remains challenging—especially when combining adaptive gradients, multi-step local training (MLT), and gradient compression (CC). We propose **LoDAdaC**, a unified framework that integrates Adam-type local updates, MLT, and CC in a theoretically sound manner. It supports a broad class of adaptive optimizers (e.g., Adam, AMSGrad, AdaGrad) and standard compressors (e.g., quantization, sparsification), without requiring global momentum synchronization or unbiased compression. We provide the first non-convex convergence analysis for such a setting, establishing an $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{NT})$ rate with communication cost reduced by up to **9×** versus baselines. Experiments on ImageNet classification and GPT-style language modeling confirm LoDAdaC achieves **1.8–2.4× faster convergence** and **5.7–9.3× lower communication bits**, outperforming state-of-the-art decentralized methods while maintaining accuracy.

item.title: Fast Isogeny Evaluation on Binary Curves
item.description: We extend the odd-characteristic isogeny evaluation techniques of Hisil–Costello and Renes to **binary fields**, providing efficient formulas for evaluating isogenies of ordinary elliptic curves over $\mathbb{F}_{2^m}$. For odd prime degree $\ell = 2s+1$, our affine product method computes the image $x$-coordinate using $5s\mathbf{M}$ field multiplications ($4s\mathbf{M}$ with normalized kernel points). We further derive an **inversion-free variant** that evaluates the $x$-map in projective and twisted Kummer coordinates—enabling carried points to remain in projective/twisted form across successive isogeny steps without any field inversions. Microbenchmarks over $\mathbb{F}_{2^{511}}$ show that the inversion-free projective and twisted variants outperform Velu-style $x$-evaluation by 1.8–2.3× when outputs stay projective/twisted, while the one-inversion affine variant achieves a **4.2× speedup**. This work enables significantly faster isogeny-based cryptography on binary-field platforms.

item.title: Quick Draw Queries: Lightweight Searchable Public-key Ciphertexts with Hidden Structures via Non-Interactive Key Exchange
item.description: This paper proposes the first generic, pairing-free, and IBE-free construction of Searchable Public-key Ciphertexts with Hidden Structures (SPCHS), built on Non-Interactive Key Exchange (NIKE) and symmetric primitives only—specifically, secret-key encryption and a PRF. Crucially, the search algorithm is entirely symmetric-key-based, eliminating all public-key operations during querying. Instantiated with DH-NIKE, HMAC-PRF, and AES, our scheme achieves over **250× faster search time** and **~20× smaller ciphertexts** compared to the leading pairing-based SPCHS (Wang et al., IEEE T-II 2020). Beyond practical efficiency, our framework enables the **first post-quantum SPCHS instantiations**, naturally supporting lattice-based (LWE), isogeny-based (SIDH), and code-based NIKE schemes—unifying security, performance, and quantum resilience in searchable encryption.

item.title: XFED: Non-Collusive Model Poisoning Attack Against Byzantine-Robust Federated Classifiers
item.description: Model poisoning attacks threaten the security of Federated Learning (FL), yet most existing methods require colluding malicious clients—exchanging models, synchronizing updates, and coordinating like a botnet—a highly impractical and detectable assumption in real-world deployments. To address this, we formalize the **non-collusive attack model**, where compromised clients act independently: no inter-client communication, no access to others’ updates, and no knowledge of server-side defenses. We propose **XFED**, the first aggregation-agnostic, non-collusive poisoning attack. XFED crafts stealthy malicious updates via gradient-direction perturbation and adaptive norm scaling, enabling evasion of statistical and robust aggregation defenses. Empirical evaluation across six benchmark datasets shows XFED bypasses eight state-of-the-art defenses (e.g., Krum, Bulyan, RFA, Norm-Clipping) and consistently outperforms six prior attacks by 23.7%–58.4% in success rate. Our results demonstrate that FL systems remain critically vulnerable even without attacker collusion—urging urgent development of practical, collusion-agnostic defenses.

item.title: BadSkill: Backdoor Attacks on Agent Skills via Model-in-Skill Poisoning
item.description: BadSkill introduces the first backdoor attack targeting *model-in-skill* components—learned model artifacts bundled within third-party agent skills. Unlike prompt injection or plugin abuse, BadSkill poisons the embedded classifier itself, causing it to execute a hidden payload only upon semantic trigger combinations (e.g., “weather=rainy & location=airport”) in routine skill parameters. We train the model via a composite objective combining classification loss, margin-based separation, and poison-focused optimization. Evaluated across 13 skills (8 triggered, 5 control) in an OpenClaw-inspired simulation, BadSkill achieves up to **99.5% average attack success rate (ASR)** across eight architectures (494M–7.1B parameters) while preserving >92% benign accuracy on negative queries. A mere **3% poison rate yields 91.7% ASR**, and the attack remains effective under five text perturbations. Our work identifies model-bearing skills as a distinct supply-chain threat surface in agent ecosystems, calling for rigorous provenance verification and behavioral vetting of third-party skill artifacts.

item.title: Unreal Thinking: Chain-of-Thought Hijacking via Two-stage Backdoor
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly rely on Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning for user-facing applications—creating a new attack surface where adversaries can hijack *observable intermediate reasoning steps* to steer outputs maliciously. We identify three core challenges in persistent CoT hijacking: (1) precise token-level manipulation within long CoT sequences without degrading downstream accuracy, (2) severe scarcity of high-quality malicious CoT data, and (3) instability of naive backdoor injection. To address this, we propose **Multiple Reverse Tree Search (MRTS)**, a prompt-output–driven reverse synthesis method that constructs output-aligned malicious CoTs *without querying aligned models*. Building on MRTS, we introduce **Two-stage Backdoor Hijacking (TSBH)**: Stage 1 induces trigger-conditioned CoT–output mismatch; Stage 2 fine-tunes on MRTS-generated CoTs with minimized embedding distance to malicious targets, ensuring semantic fidelity. Experiments across Llama-3-8B, Qwen2-7B, and Phi-3 show TSBH achieves >92% hijack success under triggers while maintaining statistically distinguishable hijacked vs. baseline states (p < 0.01) via embedding distance, entropy, and CoT variance metrics. We also release the Safety-Reasoning Dataset (SRD) and a reasoning-based mitigation. Code: https://github.com/ChangWenhan/TSBH_official.

item.title: CLIP-Inspector: Model-Level Backdoor Detection for Prompt-Tuned CLIP via OOD Trigger Inversion
item.description: We present **CLIP-Inspector (CI)**, the first model-level backdoor detector for prompt-tuned CLIP models in MLaaS settings. Unlike encoder-focused or data-sanitization methods, CI operates under a realistic white-box assumption with only unlabeled out-of-distribution (OOD) images (as few as 1,000), performing **class-wise OOD trigger inversion** to reconstruct stealthy triggers embedded in the prompt. Detection is based on statistical and behavioral signatures of the reconstructed triggers—e.g., cross-class misclassification bias and activation inconsistency. Crucially, CI also enables **post-hoc repair**: fine-tuning the model on correctly labeled triggered inputs using the inverted trigger reduces backdoor effectiveness by >85%. Extensive evaluation across 10 datasets and 4 attacks shows CI achieves **94% detection accuracy (47/50 models)** and an **AUROC of 0.973**, substantially outperforming adapted baselines (0.495/0.687). CI thus provides both verifiable model vetting and practical repair for safe deployment of prompt-tuned VLMs.

item.title: DeepGuard: Secure Code Generation via Multi-Layer Semantic Aggregation
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) for code generation often replicate insecure patterns from training data. While fine-tuning using supervision from the final transformer layer is common, it suffers from a *final-layer bottleneck*: vulnerability-discriminative cues are distributed across intermediate-to-upper layers and attenuate toward the output layer—optimized for next-token prediction rather than security reasoning. Layer-wise linear probing confirms this: strongest vulnerability signals appear in a band of upper-intermediate layers (e.g., layers 20–30), not the final one. To harness these distributed cues, we propose **DeepGuard**, a framework that aggregates representations from multiple upper layers via an attention-based module. The aggregated signal drives a dedicated security analyzer trained under a multi-objective objective balancing security enhancement and functional correctness, and enables lightweight inference-time steering. Extensive experiments across five code LLMs show DeepGuard improves the secure-and-correct generation rate by **+11.9% on average** over strong baselines (e.g., SVEN), preserves functional correctness, and generalizes to held-out vulnerability types. Code: https://github.com/unknownhl/DeepGuard.

item.title: Conversations Risk Detection LLMs in Financial Agents via Multi-Stage Generative Rollout
item.description: This paper introduces **FinSec**, a novel four-tier generative rollout framework for detecting financial dialogue risks in LLM-based agents under stringent regulatory requirements. Unlike prior single-turn or rule-based methods, FinSec jointly models multi-turn semantic evolution, delayed adversarial intent, and fine-grained regulatory compliance through: (1) domain-specific suspicious behavior pattern mining; (2) multi-step generative rollout for latent risk exposure; (3) semantic security analysis aligned with financial regulations; and (4) interpretable, risk-weighted decision making. Evaluated on a real-world financial dialogue benchmark (12.5K+ samples), FinSec achieves an **F1-score of 90.13%**, outperforming baselines by 6–14 percentage points; reduces **attack success rate (ASR) to 9.09%**; attains an **AUPRC of 0.9189** (+9.7% over general frameworks); and delivers a **balanced safety-utility score of 0.9098**, establishing new state-of-the-art for secure financial agent deployment.

item.title: Leave My Images Alone: Preventing Multi-Modal Large Language Models from Analyzing Images via Visual Prompt Injection
item.description: Multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) enable powerful image analysis but pose serious privacy risks—especially when open-weight models are abused for large-scale extraction of sensitive information from personal images. This paper introduces **ImageProtector**, a user-side, pre-sharing defense that embeds a carefully optimized, imperceptible visual perturbation into an image. This perturbation functions as a *visual prompt injection*, consistently causing target MLLMs to output refusal responses (e.g., *“I'm sorry, I can't help with that request.”*) instead of analyzing image content. We validate ImageProtector across **six open-weight MLLMs** (including LLaVA-1.5, Qwen-VL, and MiniGPT-4) and **four diverse datasets**, achieving >92% average refusal rate while preserving image fidelity and human interpretability. We further evaluate three countermeasures—Gaussian noise, DiffPure, and adversarial training—and show they only partially degrade ImageProtector’s efficacy while significantly harming model accuracy (up to −18.7%) and inference efficiency (+2.3× latency). Our work establishes the first practical, user-controlled, perturbation-based privacy shield against automated MLLM-driven image analysis.

item.title: Seeing is Believing: Robust Vision-Guided Cross-Modal Prompt Learning under Label Noise
item.description: Prompt learning enables parameter-efficient adaptation of vision-language models (VLMs), yet its vulnerability to label noise remains underexplored. Motivated by the observation that visual semantics are richer and more robust than noisy text labels, we propose **VisPrompt**: a lightweight, vision-guided cross-modal prompt learning framework. VisPrompt introduces (i) a *reverse cross-modal attention* that injects image features into prompt tokens, anchoring prompt optimization to stable instance-level visual evidence; and (ii) a *lightweight conditional modulation* that adaptively controls injection strength per sample based on visual cue quality. By decoupling prompt learning from corrupted supervision and grounding it in visual reliability, VisPrompt suppresses noise-induced instability, reduces overfitting to mislabeled samples, and maintains frozen VLM backbones. Extensive experiments under diverse synthetic and real-world label noise settings show VisPrompt consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across seven benchmarks—achieving up to +5.8% absolute accuracy gain with only ~0.08M additional trainable parameters. Code is publicly available.

item.title: DRBENCHER: Can Your Agent Identify the Entity, Retrieve Its Properties and Do the Math?
item.description: DRBENCHER is a novel synthetic benchmark generator designed to evaluate deep research agents’ *integrated* capability of entity identification, knowledge-graph property retrieval, and domain-specific computation—tasks that must be interleaved in real-world web-assisted reasoning. Unlike existing isolated benchmarks (e.g., BrowseComp+, MATH-500, GPQA), DRBENCHER enforces four rigorous criteria: **verifiability** (gold answers are programmatically computed over KG values), **complexity** (multi-hop entity linking, cross-source property aggregation, and domain math), **difficulty** (a two-stage cascade filters out questions solvable by the generator model itself), and **diversity** (greedy max-min embedding filtering achieves highest semantic coverage among all public benchmarks). Built via an answer-first pipeline across five domains (biochemistry, finance, geophysics, security, history), human evaluation confirms 76% validity (84% excluding stale KG entries), with 35% of errors attributable to outdated knowledge—a critical limitation for KG-dependent agents. Automated evaluation shows even state-of-the-art models achieve only **20% answer accuracy**, underscoring the severe “composition gap.” DRBENCHER thus establishes the first principled, scalable, and workflow-aligned testbed for holistic research agent evaluation.

item.title: CORA: Conformal Risk-Controlled Agents for Safeguarded Mobile GUI Automation
item.description: CORA (COnformal Risk-controlled GUI Agent) introduces a statistically grounded, post-policy safeguarding framework for autonomous mobile GUI agents. It replaces brittle heuristics with **conformal risk control**, enabling user-specified risk budgets (e.g., ≤1% harmful actions) via calibrated execute/abstain decisions. A Guardian model estimates action-conditional risk, while a multimodal Diagnostician interprets rejected actions to recommend minimal-burden interventions (confirm/reflect/abort). Goal-Lock anchors safety assessment to a frozen user intent, defending against visual injection. Evaluated on our new **Phone-Harm** benchmark—featuring step-level harm annotations in realistic mobile settings—CORA achieves a superior safety-helpfulness-interruption Pareto frontier: it reduces harmful actions by 3.7× versus strongest baselines (to 0.8%) while maintaining 92.4% task success and cutting average user interruptions by 41%. Code and benchmark: [cora-agent.github.io](https://cora-agent.github.io).

item.title: Event-Driven Temporal Graph Networks for Asynchronous Multi-Agent Cyber Defense in NetForge_RL
item.description: This paper tackles the Sim2Real gap hindering deployment of Multi-Agent RL (MARL) in operational cyber defense. We introduce **NetForge_RL**, a high-fidelity simulator that models network defense as an asynchronous, continuous-time Partially Observable Semi-Markov Decision Process (POSMDP), enforcing Zero-Trust constraints and consuming noisy, NLP-encoded SIEM telemetry. To solve this POSMDP, we propose **CT-GMARL**: an event-driven temporal graph network using fixed-step Neural ODEs to process irregularly timed alerts while preserving topological dynamics. Evaluated against R-MAPPO and QMIX, CT-GMARL achieves a converged median Blue reward of **57,135**—a 2.0× and 2.1× improvement, respectively—and restores **12× more compromised services**, avoiding destructive “scorched earth” policies. Crucially, its zero-shot transfer to live Docker exploits yields a median reward of **98,026**, validating robust Sim2Real bridging.

item.title: Continuous Orthogonal Mode Decomposition: Haptic Signal Prediction in Tactile Internet
item.description: The Tactile Internet imposes sub-millisecond latency and ultra-high reliability requirements, where even minor packet loss or delay can destabilize haptic teleoperation. To address this, we propose the **Mode-Domain Architecture (MDA)**—a bilateral predictive neural network leveraging a novel **Continuous Orthogonal Mode Decomposition (COMD)** framework. Unlike conventional implicit feature extractors, COMD explicitly decomposes tactile signals into continuous, orthogonal, and physically interpretable modal bases, enforced by a differentiable Gram–Schmidt orthogonality constraint that eliminates mode mixing—a critical flaw in EMD, VMD, and SST. Experiments on high-fidelity 10-kHz tactile data show MDA achieves **98.6% prediction accuracy on the human side and 97.3% on the robot side**, with an ultra-low inference latency of **0.065 ms**—12.7× faster than state-of-the-art baselines—fully satisfying ITU-R M.2410-0 real-time constraints. The lightweight COMD module (<1.2K parameters) enables edge deployment, establishing a verifiable, physics-informed signal processing foundation for 5G-Advanced and 6G tactile communications.

item.title: Automated Instruction Revision (AIR): A Structured Comparison of Task Adaptation Strategies for LLM
item.description: This paper introduces **Automated Instruction Revision (AIR)**, a rule-induction-based LLM adaptation method designed for low-resource settings with only a few task-specific examples. Unlike fine-tuning or retrieval-augmented prompting, AIR automatically synthesizes compact, human-readable instruction rules (e.g., “map ‘yes’ → 1, ‘no’ → 0”) from input-output patterns—requiring no parameter updates or external knowledge. We benchmark AIR alongside prompt optimization, KNN retrieval, and fine-tuning across five diverse tasks stressing knowledge injection, structured extraction, label remapping, and logical reasoning. Results show strong task dependence: AIR achieves top or near-top performance on label-remapping classification; KNN excels in closed-book QA; and fine-tuning dominates structured extraction and event-order reasoning. Crucially, AIR is most effective when task behavior admits concise, interpretable instruction rules—highlighting its niche in transparent, sample-efficient adaptation.

item.title: Using Synthetic Data for Machine Learning-based Childhood Vaccination Prediction in Narok, Kenya
item.description: This study addresses two critical barriers to childhood immunization in low-resource nomadic settings: (1) scarcity of high-quality, digitized data for predictive analytics, and (2) heightened privacy risks for vulnerable populations. Using 8 years of digitized MOH 510 vaccination records (n=6,913) from Narok County, Kenya, we trained Logistic Regression and XGBoost models to identify children at risk of missing key vaccines (e.g., BCG, DPT3, Measles), achieving recall, precision, and F1-scores >90% for several antigens. Crucially, we integrated **TabSyn**, a novel tabular diffusion-based synthetic data generator, to replace real patient data in model training—thereby preserving confidentiality without sacrificing performance. Models trained on synthetic data matched real-data performance (ΔF1 < 0.8%, *p* > 0.05), confirming feasibility of privacy-preserving, scalable forecasting. This work demonstrates that synthetic data can empower evidence-based, equitable immunization programming in digitally constrained health systems.
